Yes — in 2022 3M had $9,178 million of net property, plant and equipment and capital expenditures of $1,749 million.

Operating margin declined in FY2022 primarily due to substantial net costs for significant litigation (including Combat Arms Earplugs and PFAS-related charges, including a $0.8 billion PFAS manufacturing impairment and additional commitments at the Zwijndrecht, Belgium site), Russia exit charges, higher raw material and logistics costs, manufacturing productivity headwinds and declines in disposable respirator demand, which together increased cost of sales and SG&A. These headwinds were partially offset by a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain on the Food Safety split-off and by benefits from strong pricing, spending discipline and prior restructuring actions.

No — 3M’s quick ratio at June 30, 2023 = (Cash $4,258 + Marketable securities $56 + Accounts receivable $4,947) / Current liabilities $10,936 ≈ 0.85, below 1. The quick ratio may understate 3M’s liquidity because the company reports $4.3 billion of cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, had an undrawn $4.25 billion revolving credit facility (May 2023, amended July 2023), a commercial paper program (with $1.8 billion outstanding), and states that “3M maintains a strong liquidity profile.”
1.500% Notes due 2026 (MMM26, New York Stock Exchange); 1.750% Notes due 2030 (MMM30, New York Stock Exchange); 1.500% Notes due 2031 (MMM31, New York Stock Exchange).

Yes. 3M declared and paid dividends of $1.50 per share in each of the first and second quarters of 2023 (vs. $1.49 in the same quarters of 2022), and the company marked its 65th consecutive year of dividend increases.

No — Adobe’s operating margin was 34.6% in FY2022 (Operating income $6,098M ÷ Revenue $17,606M), down from 36.8% in FY2021 (Operating income $5,802M ÷ Revenue $15,785M). It had improved from 32.9% in FY2020 to 36.8% in FY2021 but then declined in FY2022.
Yes — Free cash flow conversion improved to approximately 155% in FY2022 (FCF ≈ $7,396M / Net income $4,756M) from approximately 143% in FY2021 (FCF ≈ $6,882M / Net income $4,822M).

Inventory turnover for FY2022 ≈ 12.1× (Cost of sales $10,069M ÷ average inventory (($1,055M + $604M)/2 = $829.5M)).  
Conventional inventory-management interpretation is limited because inventory primarily consists of fuel and other raw materials and spare parts and supplies used in generation, so turnover mainly reflects fuel consumption and maintenance cycles rather than retail-style stock sales.

The 8-K reported the substitution of Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. for Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. as issuer under the 2016 and 2019 Indentures and the assumption by the Substitute Issuer of the Former Issuer’s covenants with respect to the 3.625% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2026 and the 4.500% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2028.
AMCOR's quick ratio improved slightly from approximately 0.67 in FY2022 to approximately 0.69 in FY2023.

FY2023 — On August 1, 2022 Amcor acquired a Czech Republic flexible‑packaging manufacturing company for $59 million; on March 17, 2023 it acquired a medical device packaging manufacturing site in Shanghai, China for $60 million (including $20 million contingent consideration); and on May 31, 2023 it acquired a New Zealand manufacturer of automated protein packaging machines for $45 million (including $13 million contingent consideration). FY2022 — No acquisitions completed in fiscal year 2022 are disclosed in this Form 10‑K. FY2021 — No acquisitions completed in fiscal year 2021 are disclosed in this Form 10‑K.

No — consolidated gross profit margin decreased to 18.5% in FY2023 from 19.4% in FY2022, with gross profit down $95 million to $2,725 million.

Amcor's restructuring liability at December 31, 2022 is an accrual of $93 million composed of employee costs ($81m), fixed-asset related costs ($2m) and other costs ($10m) arising from restructuring activities primarily related to the Russia‑Ukraine conflict (and prior Bemis integration). Its purpose is to fund expected employee-related charges, fixed-asset and other restructuring expenses; these accruals are recorded in other current and other non‑current liabilities and the company expects the majority to be paid within the next twelve months.

Yes — AMD’s quick ratio for FY22 is approximately (Cash and cash equivalents $4,835 + Short-term investments $1,020 + Accounts receivable, net $4,126) / Current liabilities $6,369 ≈ 1.57, indicating its quick assets exceed its current liabilities. The company also states that its cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments together with its Revolving Credit Facility and cash flows from operations will be sufficient to fund operations over the next 12 months and beyond.

AMD's major products and services as of FY22 are server microprocessors (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs), data processing units (DPUs), Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) and Adaptive System‑on‑Chip (SoC) products for data centers; CPUs, accelerated processing units (APUs) that integrate CPUs and GPUs, and chipsets for desktop and notebook personal computers; discrete GPUs, semi‑custom SoC products and development services; embedded CPUs, GPUs, APUs, FPGAs and Adaptive SoC products; and, from time to time, sales or licenses of portions of its intellectual property (IP) portfolio.

The increase was driven by a 64% rise in Data Center revenue primarily from higher EPYC server processor sales, a 21% increase in Gaming revenue mainly from higher semi‑custom product sales, and a significant increase in Embedded revenue due to inclusion of Xilinx embedded product sales, partially offset by a 10% decrease in Client revenue due to lower processor shipments from a weak PC market and inventory correction actions.

AMD’s operating margin change in FY22 was primarily driven by amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets associated with the Xilinx acquisition and higher operating expenses, including increased research and development and marketing, general and administrative expenses from acquisitions and organic growth.  Specifically, “All Other” included $3.5 billion of amortization of acquisition-related intangibles, $1.1 billion of stock-based compensation expense and $452 million of acquisition-related costs, and gross margin declined primarily due to amortization of Xilinx-related intangible assets.
Operating activities — net cash provided of $3,565 million.

United States; outside the United States, the United Kingdom (UK), the European Union (EU), Australia, Japan, Canada and Mexico.

No — American Express’s operating (pretax) margin declined in 2022: total revenues net of interest expense rose 25% to $52,862 million while pretax income fell to $9,585 million from $10,689 million in 2021. The report attributes the decline to higher provisions for credit losses and increased expenses — including higher card member rewards, net losses on Amex Ventures and higher compensation and service costs — which offset revenue growth.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express because it is a “spend‑centric” integrated payments company that generates revenues primarily from discount revenue, interest income, card fees, processed revenue and service fees rather than from selling goods, and its principal costs (Card Member rewards, Card Member services, business development and other variable expenses) are volume‑driven and correlated to billed business.  Profitability changes in FY2022 were driven by billed‑business growth (which increased discount revenue), higher net interest income from growth in Card Member loans, and movements in provisions for credit losses and variable customer‑engagement expenses, rather than by a traditional gross‑margin measure.
It decreased by 3.0 percentage points (from 24.6% in FY2021 to 21.6% in FY2022).

No — American Water Works had negative working capital at December 31, 2022: current assets $1,250 million minus current liabilities $2,811 million = a deficit of $1,561 million. Working capital can be of limited usefulness for this regulated utility because current liabilities often exceed current assets due to debt maturities and use of short‑term borrowings (commercial paper and revolving credit), and the Company manages short‑term liquidity through those financing mechanisms and operating cash flows.

At the consolidated level (and for the Domestic segment) gross profit rates were 22.4% (2021), 22.5% (2022) and 21.4% (2023) (Domestic 22.5%, 22.4%, 21.3%), showing annual fluctuations of about 1.1 percentage points and thus historically consistent within roughly 2%. However, the International segment moved from 21.5% (2021) to 23.9% (2022) to 23.0% (2023), which included a greater-than-2% swing between 2021 and 2022.

Yes — Commercial Airplanes; Defense, Space & Security; and Global Services.

Yes — Boeing disclosed a number of ongoing legal proceedings in FY2022, notably matters arising from the Lion Air Flight 610 and Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 737 MAX accidents (including the DOJ Deferred Prosecution Agreement, an SEC settlement and a shareholder derivative settlement) and an arbitration dispute with Embraer.

Yes — Boeing’s cost of sales as a percentage of revenues declined to 94.7% in 2022 from 95.2% in 2021 (implying gross margin improved to about 5.3% from about 4.8%). However, gross margin is a limited metric for Boeing because its Commercial Airplanes business and many long‑term contracts use program/accounting estimates (program accounting quantity, long‑term contract percentage‑of‑completion, reach‑forward losses and cumulative catch‑up adjustments), so reported gross margins can change materially based on revisions to estimated revenues, costs, delivery timing and abnormal production or one‑time charges.
Commercial airlines worldwide and the U.S. government (including other governments through foreign military sales).

Yes — the commercial aircraft market and airline industry have historically been cyclical and market conditions have a significant impact on demand for Boeing’s commercial aircraft and related services.

Boeing forecasts gradually increasing 787 production to 5 per month in 2023; resuming 777X production in 2023 after a pause; and gradually increasing 737 production rates beyond the 31-per-month level based on market demand and supply chain capacity.
Boeing’s effective tax rate was approximately -0.6% in FY2022 compared with approximately -14.8% in FY2021.
It increased by 2.7 percentage points (from 20.2% in FY2021 to 22.9% in FY2022).
